Appointed 'Consul of the Philosophers' (Hypatos ton Philosophon), effectively head of the University of Constantinople.
Under pressure, he enters a monastery and takes the monastic name Michael.
Recalled to the imperial court by Empress Theodora.
Composes the 'Chronographia,' his most famous historical work.
Believed to have died around this year, though the exact date is uncertain.
Author of the Chronographia
Wrote a detailed and personal history of 14 Byzantine rulers, which remains a primary source for the 11th-century Byzantine Empire.
Revival of Philosophy
As 'Consul of the Philosophers,' he revitalized the University of Constantinople and championed the study of ancient Greek philosophy, particularly Plato.
Influential Political Advisor
Served as a high-ranking advisor to multiple emperors and empresses, shaping Byzantine politics for several decades through his intellect and diplomacy.
What makes Psellos's 'Chronographia' so special?

Unlike other histories of the time, the 'Chronographia' is a personal memoir, filled with vivid character sketches, court intrigue, and Psellos's own opinions and justifications. It reads more like a modern political biography than a dry chronicle.
Was he a monk or a politician?

He was both. He was forced to become a monk for a short period due to political pressure but was quickly recalled to court. He balanced his scholarly and religious life with a highly active and ambitious political career.
What was his philosophical focus?
Psellos was a Neoplatonist. He sought to synthesize Christian theology with the philosophy of Plato and his followers, believing that ancient wisdom could illuminate Christian faith.
Born in the village of Mishaninskaya (later renamed Lomonosovo) in Arkhangelsk Governorate, Russia.
At age 19, leaves his village and walks to Moscow to enroll in the Slavic Greek Latin Academy.
Is sent to Germany to study chemistry, physics, and metallurgy at the University of Marburg.
Returns to Russia and is appointed as an adjunct professor at the St. Petersburg Academy of Sciences.
Establishes the first research and teaching chemical laboratory in Russia.
Co-founds Moscow State University with his patron, Ivan Shuvalov, based on his proposed plan.
Observes the transit of Venus and is the first to discover and document the presence of an atmosphere on the planet.
Dies of pneumonia in St. Petersburg and is buried in the Lazarevskoe Cemetery.
Law of Conservation of Mass
He stated in a letter to Leonhard Euler (1748) that in a chemical reaction, the mass of the reactants equals the mass of the products, a precursor to the law of conservation of mass.
Moscow State University
He conceived of and, with Ivan Shuvalov, founded Russia's first university in Moscow in 1755, which now bears his name.
Atmosphere of Venus
During the 1761 transit of Venus, he was the first person to hypothesize the existence of an atmosphere on Venus based on his observation of the Lomonosov's arc.
Russian Grammar Reform
Published 'Russian Grammar' (1755), which standardized the Russian literary language by distinguishing between High, Middle, and Low styles, shaping modern Russian.
Revival of Mosaics
He rediscovered and revived the ancient art of mosaics, establishing a factory to produce colored glass and create large-scale mosaic artworks.
Is it true Lomonosov walked all the way to Moscow?

Yes, it is a famous and true part of his life story. In the winter of 1730, at age 19, he left his village with a fish-cart train and traveled over 1,000 kilometers (more than 600 miles) to Moscow to pursue an education he could not get at home.
What is Lomonosov's most important scientific contribution?

It is difficult to choose one, but his formulation of a version of the law of conservation of mass in chemistry and his discovery of Venus's atmosphere are among his most significant. His founding of Moscow University is his most lasting contribution to education.
Why is he called the 'Father of Russian Science'?

He earned this title for his foundational work in establishing Russian scientific institutions, including Russia's first chemical lab and Moscow University, and for his own wide-ranging discoveries that proved Russians could excel at the highest levels of scientific inquiry.
Was Lomonosov also a poet?

Yes, he was a major figure in 18th-century Russian literature. He wrote odes, poems, and tragedies, and his work on Russian grammar helped to standardize the literary language used by poets and writers who followed him, including Pushkin.
Born in Hadiach, Poltava Governorate, Russian Empire.
Studied at Kyiv University and became a prominent member of the 'Hromada' student society.
Became a docent (lecturer) in ancient history at Kyiv University.
Dismissed from his post following the Ems Ukaz, which banned the Ukrainian language, and went into political exile.
Founded the influential Ukrainian-language political journal 'Hromada' in Geneva, Switzerland.
Accepted a professorship at the University of Sofia in Bulgaria.
Died in Sofia, Bulgaria, and was buried there.
Father of Ukrainian Socialism
He was one of the first and most influential socialist thinkers in Ukraine, blending socialist ideals with national aspirations for autonomy.
Hromada Journal
Founded and edited the influential political journal 'Hromada' in Geneva, which became a key platform for Ukrainian political thought in exile.
Federalist Theory
Developed a comprehensive political theory for the federal reorganization of the Russian and Austro-Hungarian Empires to grant autonomy to nations like Ukraine.
Intellectual Mentorship
He was a major intellectual influence on the next generation of Ukrainian leaders, including his niece, the famed writer Lesia Ukrainka.
What was the Ems Ukaz and how did it affect Drahomanov?

It was an 1876 decree by Tsar Alexander II that severely restricted the use of the Ukrainian language in print. Drahomanov's opposition to this and similar policies led to his dismissal from Kyiv University and forced him into exile.
Was Drahomanov a nationalist?

His views were nuanced. He was a patriot who championed Ukrainian rights and culture, but he was critical of chauvinistic or separatist nationalism. He advocated for a federal system where Ukraine would have autonomy alongside other nations, rather than complete separation.
What was his relationship with Lesia Ukrainka?

He was her maternal uncle and a profound intellectual mentor. His political and social ideas, shared through extensive correspondence, heavily influenced her literary works and her own political development.
Why did he move from Geneva to Sofia?

While Geneva was a center for his publishing, his political circle fractured due to ideological disagreements. In 1889, he accepted a professorship at the University of Sofia, which provided him with academic stability in his later years.
Born in Chełm, Congress Poland, Russian Empire.
Appointed Professor of History at Lviv University in Austria-Hungary.
Publishes the first volume of his magnum opus, 'History of Ukraine-Rus''.
Elected head of the Central Rada (Council) in Kyiv following the February Revolution.
Elected President of the Ukrainian People's Republic.
Lives in exile in Vienna and Prague after the collapse of the republic.
Returns to Soviet Ukraine to continue his academic work.
Arrested by the Soviet secret police (OGPU) and exiled to Moscow.
Dies under mysterious circumstances in Kislovodsk, Russia.
History of Ukraine-Rus'
Authored a monumental 10-volume history that established a continuous narrative for Ukraine, separate from Russian and Polish history.
First President of Ukraine
Served as the head of state of the first modern independent Ukrainian state, the Ukrainian People's Republic.
Head of Shevchenko Scientific Society
Transformed the society in Lviv into a premier Ukrainian-language scholarly institution, effectively a proto-academy of sciences.
Father of Modern Ukrainian Identity
His academic and political work was foundational in shaping modern Ukrainian national consciousness and historiography.
Was Hrushevsky a socialist?
Yes, he was a moderate socialist and a leading member of the Ukrainian Party of Socialist-Revolutionaries, which advocated for national autonomy, democracy, and land reform for the peasantry.
Why did he return to Soviet Ukraine in 1924?

He returned under the Soviet 'Ukrainization' policy, hoping he could contribute to Ukrainian cultural and academic life. He believed he could do more for Ukraine from within, a decision that ultimately proved tragic as Stalin's regime intensified its repression.
What is Hrushevsky's legacy in Ukraine today?

He is revered as a national hero and a founding father of the modern Ukrainian state. His portrait is on the 50-hryvnia banknote, and his work is considered the cornerstone of Ukrainian historical studies and national identity.
Did he finish his 10-volume history?

He completed and published all ten volumes, covering Ukrainian history from ancient times to the 1660s. His work provided a comprehensive and scholarly basis for Ukraine's claim to a long and distinct history.
페르가나 계곡의 안디잔에서 출생.
12세의 나이로 페르가나의 왕위에 오름.
처음으로 사마르칸트를 점령했으나 곧 잃음.
카불을 점령하고 새로운 거점으로 삼음.
제1차 파니파트 전투에서 이브라힘 로디를 격파하고 델리를 점령.
칸와 전투에서 라지푸트 연합군을 격파하고 지배권을 확고히 함.
무굴 제국의 아그라에서 사망.
무굴 제국의 창시자
파니파트 전투에서의 승리를 통해 인도 역사상 가장 강력한 제국 중 하나의 기틀을 마련했습니다.
바부르나마의 저자
이슬람 문학 최초의 자서전 중 하나를 집필하여 자신의 삶과 시대에 대한 귀중한 통찰력을 제공했습니다.
인도 화약 전쟁의 선구자
야전 포병과 화기를 전략적으로 사용한 것은 인도에서의 군사적 승리에 핵심적인 요인이었습니다.
'바부르'라는 이름은 무슨 뜻인가요?
'바부르'라는 이름은 '호랑이'를 의미하는 페르시아어에서 유래했으며, 전투에서의 그의 용맹함과 용기 때문에 얻은 별명입니다.
사마르칸트는 왜 그에게 그토록 중요했나요?
사마르칸트는 그의 조상인 티무르(타메를란)의 웅장한 수도였으며, 그곳을 되찾는 것은 티무르 제국의 정당한 계승권을 상징하는 평생의 집념이었습니다.
바부르나마는 역사적으로 정확한가요?
바부르나마는 당대로서는 놀라울 정도로 솔직하고 정확한 것으로 평가받습니다. 그의 관점에서 쓰였지만, 그의 승리뿐만 아니라 실패와 약점까지 상세히 기술하여 귀중한 역사적 자료가 됩니다.
Born as a 'porphyrogennitos' (born in the purple), a son of Emperor Romanos II and Empress Theophano.
Becomes senior emperor after the death of John I Tzimiskes, but real power is held by the eunuch Basil Lekapenos.
Faces and eventually crushes a series of large-scale internal rebellions led by the powerful generals Bardas Skleros and Bardas Phokas.
Forms a crucial alliance with Vladimir I of Kiev, receiving 6,000 Norse mercenaries (the foundation of the Varangian Guard) in exchange for his sister Anna's hand in marriage.
Achieves a decisive victory over the Bulgarian army at the Battle of Kleidion.
Completes the conquest of the First Bulgarian Empire, annexing it fully into the Byzantine Empire.
Dies while planning a military expedition to reclaim Sicily, leaving the empire at its territorial and financial zenith.
Conquest of Bulgaria
After a brutal, decades-long war, he completely subjugated the First Bulgarian Empire, the empire's main European foe for centuries.
Formation of the Varangian Guard
He established the legendary Varangian Guard, an elite unit of Norse mercenaries who served as the emperor's personal, incorruptible bodyguards.
Fiscal Consolidation
Through austere living, land reforms targeting the powerful aristocracy, and efficient taxation, he left the imperial treasury with a massive surplus upon his death.
Eastern Campaigns
He led successful campaigns against the Fatimid Caliphate and other eastern powers, securing the empire's Anatolian frontier.
Why was he really called the 'Bulgar-Slayer'?

He earned the epithet 'Boulgaroktonos' for his relentless and brutal subjugation of the Bulgarian Empire. The name became permanently associated with him after his victory at Kleidion in 1014, following which he allegedly blinded 15,000 Bulgarian prisoners.
Did he ever marry or have children?

No. Basil II dedicated his life entirely to the state and the military. He never married and died childless, which ultimately led to a succession crisis and contributed to the decline of the Macedonian dynasty after his death.
What was his relationship with the Kievan Rus'?

He formed a pivotal alliance with Vladimir the Great. In exchange for military aid (the Varangian Guard) and Basil's sister Anna in marriage, Vladimir converted to Orthodox Christianity, leading to the Christianization of the entire Kievan Rus'.
Was he a good administrator?

Yes, he was a highly effective administrator. He curbed the power of the land-owning aristocracy, reformed the tax system to be more equitable and efficient, and practiced extreme fiscal austerity, leaving the empire incredibly wealthy.
Born in Pingyang, which is modern-day Linfen, Shanxi, China.
Departs from the Eastern Jin capital of Chang'an to travel to India in search of Buddhist scriptures.
Successfully crosses the Taklamakan Desert and the treacherous Pamir Mountains, arriving in Northwest India.
Travels extensively through northern India, studying Sanskrit and collecting scriptures, spending three years in Pataliputra.
Travels to Sri Lanka (the 'Lion Kingdom') to obtain more texts, then begins his perilous sea voyage home.
After surviving severe storms and an unplanned stop in Java, he finally arrives back in China at Laoshan, in modern Shandong.
Resides in Jiankang (modern Nanjing), where he translates the scriptures he collected and dictates his travelogue, 'A Record of Buddhist Kingdoms' (Foguo Ji).
Dies at Xin Monastery in Jingzhou at the approximate age of 85.
Pilgrimage to India
Completed a 14-year overland and sea journey from China to India and back to retrieve Buddhist scriptures at an advanced age.
A Record of Buddhist Kingdoms (Foguo Ji)
Authored one of history's most important travelogues, providing an invaluable eyewitness account of Central and South Asia in the early 5th century.
Translation of Buddhist Texts
Translated key Sanskrit texts into Chinese, most notably the Mahāsāṃghika Vinaya, which significantly influenced monastic discipline in East Asia.
Why did Faxian travel to India?

He was distressed by the incomplete and inconsistent state of the Vinaya (monastic rules) available in China and sought to obtain the original, complete versions from India, the birthplace of Buddhism.
How long did Faxian's journey take?
His entire pilgrimage, from leaving Chang'an to returning to China, lasted approximately 14-15 years, from around 399 to 414 CE.
Is Faxian's travelogue historically accurate?

Yes, 'A Record of Buddhist Kingdoms' (Foguo Ji) is considered a remarkably accurate and valuable primary source for the history, geography, and religious conditions of the regions he visited in the early 5th century.
Did Faxian travel alone?

No, he started his journey from Chang'an with a group of other monks, including Huijing, Daozheng, Huiying, and Huiwei. However, due to the extreme hardships of the journey, some died and others chose to stay in India, and Faxian ultimately returned to China alone from his original group.
Born in Germania, a fortified town in what is now modern Bulgaria.
Appointed as a general on the eastern frontier by Emperor Justin I.
Plays a key role in suppressing the Nika riots in Constantinople, saving Emperor Justinian's throne.
Leads the Vandalic War, conquering the Vandal Kingdom in North Africa in less than a year.
Leads the initial and most significant campaigns of the Gothic War to reconquer Italy from the Ostrogoths.
Captures the Ostrogothic capital of Ravenna and their king, Witiges.
In retirement, he is recalled to defend Constantinople from a Kutrigur Bulgar invasion, which he successfully repels.
Accused of conspiracy against Justinian, he is disgraced and briefly imprisoned before being pardoned.
Dies in Constantinople, a few months after his Emperor, Justinian.
Conquest of the Vandal Kingdom
He swiftly destroyed the Vandal Kingdom in North Africa in under a year (533-534), a feat considered a masterpiece of military planning and execution.
Reconquest of Italy
He led the initial, largely successful phase of the Gothic War, capturing Sicily, Naples, Rome, and the Gothic capital of Ravenna against numerically superior forces.
Defense of the East
He fought with distinction against the Sassanian Empire, securing the eastern frontier of the Byzantine Empire at the Battle of Dara.
Suppression of the Nika Riots
His decisive and ruthless action alongside the eunuch Narses crushed the massive urban revolt in 532, saving Justinian's throne from collapse.
Was Belisarius really blinded and forced to beg?

This is a popular legend that emerged in the Middle Ages, but there is no contemporary historical evidence to support it. He was briefly disgraced and imprisoned in 562 but his property and freedom were restored by Emperor Justinian.
Why was Emperor Justinian so suspicious of him?

Belisarius's immense popularity, stunning military successes, and the fact that the Goths offered him the Western throne made him a potential rival in the eyes of the often-paranoid Justinian and his court.
Who was the main historian who wrote about Belisarius?

His personal secretary, Procopius of Caesarea, wrote extensively about his campaigns in 'The Wars of Justinian' and offered a much more critical, scandalous view in the posthumously published 'Secret History'.
Born into a family of minor Ukrainian nobility.
Captured by the Ottomans at the Battle of Cecora and spent two years in captivity.
The "Chyhyryn affair" where Polish official Daniel Czapliński seizes his estate, leading to a personal vendetta that sparks a national rebellion.
Begins the Khmelnytsky Uprising, securing major victories at Zhovti Vody and Korsun.
Signs the Treaty of Zboriv, which recognizes the Cossack Hetmanate as an autonomous entity within the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth.
Suffers a major defeat at the Battle of Berestechko after being abandoned by his Tatar allies.
Signs the Treaty of Pereiaslav, placing the Cossack Hetmanate under the protection of the Tsar of Muscovy.
Dies in Chyhyryn, leaving behind a nascent but unstable state.
Leadership of the Khmelnytsky Uprising
Successfully organized and led a massive popular rebellion against the powerful Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth.
Establishment of the Cossack Hetmanate
Created the first semi-independent Ukrainian Cossack state, which served as a foundation for modern Ukrainian national identity.
Military Victories
Achieved a series of stunning military victories against Polish forces, demonstrating brilliant tactical and strategic leadership.
Was Khmelnytsky a hero or a villain?

He is a deeply controversial figure. To many Ukrainians, he is a national hero who founded their first modern state. To Poles and Jews, his legacy is far more negative due to the immense violence and massacres committed during his uprising.
What was the significance of the Treaty of Pereiaslav?

It was a pivotal moment. Seeking protection from Poland, Khmelnytsky allied with the Tsardom of Muscovy. This act is seen by some as a necessary evil for survival, and by others as the moment Ukraine traded Polish domination for Russian domination, leading to centuries of Russian influence.
Why did the uprising involve such widespread violence against Jews?

The Jewish population was often associated with the Polish szlachta (nobility) as leaseholders and tax collectors (arendators), making them targets of the rebels' fury against the socio-economic system. The uprising unleashed long-simmering resentments, resulting in horrific massacres.
Emerged as chieftain of the Ashina Turks, vassals of the Rouran Khaganate.
Suppressed a Tiele revolt for the Rouran and requested a Rouran princess in marriage as a reward.
The Rouran Khagan Anagui refused and insulted Bumin, sparking the Turkic rebellion.
Allied with the Western Wei, married Princess Changle, and defeated the Rouran forces. Rouran Khagan Anagui committed suicide.
Declared himself Ilig Qaghan at the Otuken capital, founding the First Turkic Khaganate.
Died shortly after founding the Khaganate. His brother Istämi was appointed Yabgu (ruler) of the western territories.
Revolt Against the Rouran
Led a successful rebellion against the Rouran Khaganate, his people's long-time overlords.
Founding the Turkic Khaganate
Established the First Turkic Khaganate in 552, initiating a new era of Turkic power in Eurasia.
Strategic Alliance with Western Wei
Secured a crucial alliance with the Chinese Western Wei dynasty, which legitimized his rule and provided military support.
Who were the Göktürks?

The Göktürks (or 'Celestial Turks') were a confederation of Turkic peoples in medieval Inner Asia, founded by Bumin Qaghan. They were the first Turkic state to use the name 'Türk' in a political context.
What does 'Qaghan' mean?
'Qaghan' (or Khagan) is a title of imperial rank in Turkic, Mongol, and other nomadic languages, equivalent to the status of an emperor.
Why was being a blacksmith significant?

For steppe nomads, blacksmiths were highly respected and even feared, believed to hold special powers over fire and metal. The Rouran Khagan's insult, calling Bumin a 'blacksmith slave', was therefore a deep social and cultural affront.
Born, the illegitimate son of Sviatoslav I of Kyiv.
Defeats and kills his half-brother Yaropolk, becoming Grand Prince of Kyiv.
Is baptized into Orthodox Christianity in Chersonesus, marries Byzantine princess Anna Porphyrogenita.
Organizes the mass baptism of the people of Kyiv in the Dnieper River, initiating the Christianization of Rus'.
Dies at Berestove, near Kyiv, after ruling for 35 years.
Unification of Kievan Rus'
He consolidated the lands of the Rus' from the Baltic to the Black Sea, unifying various East Slavic tribes under his rule.
Christianization of Kievan Rus'
His adoption of Orthodox Christianity in 988 as the state religion became a pivotal moment in Eastern European history.
Cultural and Legal Reforms
Following his conversion, he established schools, churches, and ecclesiastical courts, laying the foundation for a new Christian society.
Why is he called 'the Great'?
He is called 'the Great' for unifying the lands of Kievan Rus' and for his monumental decision to Christianize the realm, which defined its cultural and religious identity for centuries.
Was Vladimir a saint?

Yes, despite his brutal early life as a pagan, his later piety and work in establishing Christianity led to his canonization as a saint in the Orthodox Church, where he is known as 'Saint Vladimir the Equal-to-the-Apostles'.
Why did he choose Orthodoxy over other religions?

His envoys were awestruck by the beauty of the liturgy at Hagia Sophia in Constantinople. This, combined with the strategic advantage of a military and marital alliance with the powerful Byzantine Empire, made Orthodoxy the most appealing choice.
Born in Saint Petersburg, Russian Empire.
Graduates from Saint Petersburg State University.
Becomes a full member of the St. Petersburg Academy of Sciences.
Founds and becomes the first president of the Ukrainian Academy of Sciences in Kyiv.
Becomes director of the newly founded Radium Institute in Petrograd.
Publishes his seminal work, "The Biosphere," in Russian (French translation in 1929).
Publishes his final paper on the noosphere.
Dies in Moscow, Soviet Union.
Concept of the Biosphere

He redefined the biosphere as the global ecological system integrating all living beings and their relationships, including their interaction with the elements of the lithosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere.
Founding Biogeochemistry
He established biogeochemistry as a new scientific discipline, studying the chemical processes and cycles that are driven by or impact biological activity.
Concept of the Noosphere
He theorized the noosphere as the third stage in Earth's development, after the geosphere and biosphere, representing the sphere of human reason and consciousness as a geological force.
Ukrainian Academy of Sciences
He was a principal founder and the first president of the Ukrainian Academy of Sciences, establishing a major center for scientific research in Kyiv.
What is the difference between the biosphere and the noosphere?

The biosphere is the global sum of all ecosystems, the zone of life on Earth. The noosphere is Vernadsky's concept for the next evolutionary stage, where human reason and consciousness become the dominant force shaping the biosphere.
How is Vernadsky's work related to the Gaia hypothesis?

Vernadsky's concept of the biosphere as a geological force that transforms the planet predates and influenced James Lovelock's Gaia hypothesis, which similarly views the Earth as a self-regulating system. However, Vernadsky's focus was more on the chemical and physical impact of life, whereas Gaia includes the idea of homeostatic feedback loops.
